explanations.1: Kinematics focuses purely on describing the motion of objects, whereas the study of causes (forces) is known as dynamics.
explanations.2: Displacement is defined as the change in position (final position minus initial position) and includes direction, represented here by the plus sign.
explanations.3: Distance traveled is a scalar quantity representing the total length of the path taken, which is 3 km + 2 km = 5 km.
explanations.4: A vector must have both magnitude and direction, whereas scalars like temperature, height, and speed only have magnitude.
explanations.5: For scalars, a minus sign signifies a position on a scale (like Celsius) rather than a spatial direction.
explanations.6: While other units are used in various contexts, the meter is the fundamental SI unit for measuring length and displacement.
explanations.7: Average velocity is specifically the ratio of displacement ($\Delta x$) to the time interval ($\Delta t$).
explanations.8: Because the car returns to its starting point, its total displacement is zero, making the average velocity zero regardless of the distance traveled.
explanations.9: A speedometer shows the magnitude of the car's velocity at a specific moment but does not provide the direction of travel.
explanations.10: Acceleration is defined as the rate of change of velocity, which is a vector, so changes in either speed or direction constitute acceleration.
explanations.11: Acceleration is the change in velocity (m/s) per unit of time (s), resulting in meters per second squared.
explanations.12: When the signs of velocity and acceleration are opposite, the acceleration opposes the motion, causing the object to slow down (deceleration).
explanations.14: This equation is the algebraic rearrangement of the definition of acceleration for a constant rate of change.
explanations.15: Position $x = x_0 + vt = 0 + (4.00 m/s \times 120 s) = 480 m$.
explanations.17: This equation relates velocities, acceleration, and displacement without requiring a time variable.
explanations.18: Correct units in the final answer help confirm that the equation was manipulated correctly, though they do not guarantee numerical accuracy.
explanations.19: 40 m/s is approximately 150 km/h, which is physically impossible for a human runner, indicating the premise was flawed.
explanations.20: Galileo's principle states that in free-fall, gravity acts on all masses to produce the same constant acceleration ($g$).
explanations.21: While $g$ varies slightly by location, $9.80 m/s^2$ is the standard average value used for Earth.
explanations.22: Even when the rock momentarily stops at the top, gravity continues to act on it with a constant acceleration of $-g$.
explanations.24: In free-fall, an object returning to its original height will have the same speed it started with but in the opposite direction.
explanations.25: Slope is defined as rise over run ($\Delta x / \Delta t$), which is the definition of velocity.
explanations.26: A changing slope on a position-time graph indicates that velocity is changing, which means the object is accelerating.
explanations.27: The y-intercept is the value of the dependent variable (velocity) when the independent variable (time) is zero.
explanations.28: The slope of a velocity-time graph is $\Delta v / \Delta t$, which is the definition of acceleration.
explanations.29: A horizontal line has a slope of zero, and since the slope of a velocity-time graph is acceleration, the acceleration must be zero.
explanations.30: The slope of the tangent line represents the rate of change of position at that specific infinitesimal interval of time.
